title: CSS Cascade, Specificity & Inheritance
description: Read CSS conflicts the way the engine does: specificity by column, why `@layer` order outranks specificity (and how `!important` reverses it), unlayered-beats-layered, inherited vs. `initial`/`revert`/`unset`, and `:where()`'s zero specificity. Answers follow the CSS Cascade and Selectors specifications.

questions.title: An `<a>` matches both rules below — which color wins?

options.text: `blue` — one ID outranks any number of classes and types.
`red` — it matches more selectors, so it is more specific overall.
`red` — it appears later in the source, which breaks the tie.
Neither — the conflict cancels both and the color falls back to the default.

questions.explanation:
Specificity is compared **column by column, left to right**: IDs (A), then classes/attributes/pseudo-classes (B), then types/pseudo-elements (C). The first column that differs decides the winner — totals never matter.

- `#header .nav a` → **(1, 1, 1)** — one ID, one class, one type.
- `header nav a.link.active` → **(0, 2, 3)** — zero IDs, two classes, three types.

Comparing the A column first: `1 > 0`, so `#header .nav a` wins immediately and the link is **blue**. The second selector has more classes *and* more type selectors, yet a single ID outranks every lower column combined. Source order is only consulted when specificity ties — it never does here.

```css
#header .nav a           { color: blue; } /* (1,1,1) → wins on the ID column */
header nav a.link.active { color: red;  } /* (0,2,3) */
```

This is exactly why sprinkling IDs into selectors makes a stylesheet painful to override later.

questions.title: What is the specificity of `#sidebar ul li a.active`?
questions.description: Give the `(A, B, C)` triple — IDs, then classes/pseudo-classes, then types.
questions.explanation:
Sort each simple selector into its column:

- `#sidebar` → 1 ID → **A = 1**
- `.active` → 1 class → **B = 1**
- `ul`, `li`, `a` → 3 type selectors → **C = 3**

The descendant combinators (the spaces) contribute nothing. So the specificity is **(1, 1, 3)**.

The tempting mistake is the `a.active` compound: `a` is a *type* selector (column C) and `.active` is a *class* (column B), so they split into different columns rather than both landing in B. Counting `#sidebar` as a class instead of an ID would wrongly give (0, 2, 3).

questions.title: In the specificity of `nav.primary > ul li.active a:hover`, what is the B column?
questions.description: Enter the count for the B column: classes, attribute selectors, and pseudo-classes combined.
questions.explanation:
The **B** column counts classes, attribute selectors, **and pseudo-classes** together. A pseudo-class such as `:hover` lives here — not in the type column.

For `nav.primary > ul li.active a:hover`:

- A (IDs): none → 0
- B (`.primary`, `.active`, `:hover`): **3**
- C (`nav`, `ul`, `li`, `a`): 4

The child combinator `>` and the descendant spaces add nothing. The full triple is **(0, 3, 4)**, so **B = 3**. Forgetting that `:hover` counts in B (or miscounting it as a type) is a classic off-by-one.

questions.title: Which statements about the specificity of functional pseudo-classes are correct?
questions.description: Select every true statement about `:where()`, `:is()`, `:not()`, and `:nth-child()`.

options.text: `:where()` always contributes zero specificity, no matter what selectors are inside it.
`:is()` takes the specificity of its most specific argument.
`:not()` adds the specificity of its most specific argument; the `:not()` wrapper itself adds nothing extra.
`:nth-child()` contributes zero specificity, just like `:where()`.

questions.explanation:
The functional pseudo-classes were given deliberate, distinct specificity rules:

- **`:where()`** — its specificity is **replaced by zero**, however many IDs sit inside. That is what makes it ideal for low-priority, easily-overridable defaults.
- **`:is()`** — replaced by the specificity of its **most specific argument**.
- **`:not()`** — the wrapper adds nothing, but its **most specific argument** is counted.
- **`:nth-child()`** is an ordinary pseudo-class, so it contributes **(0, 1, 0)** — *not* zero. (With the `:nth-child(... of <selector>)` form it also adds that selector's specificity.)

```css
:is(#a, .b) p    { } /* (1,0,1) — most specific arg is #a */
:where(#a, .b) p { } /* (0,0,1) — :where zeroes the whole group out */
p:not(#a)        { } /* (1,0,1) — :not adds #a's (1,0,0) */
li:nth-child(2n) { } /* (0,1,1) — :nth-child is a normal pseudo-class */
```

questions.title: What color is the heading, given these cascade layers?

options.text: `green` — `components` is the later layer, and layer order is compared before specificity.
`red` — `#title` has higher specificity, which always wins.
`red` — the `#title` rule appears later in the source file.
`green` — `:where()`-style zero specificity is applied inside layers.

questions.title: With `!important` on both rules, which layer wins?

options.text: `red` — for `!important` declarations the layer order is reversed, so the earlier layer wins.
`green` — `theme` is the later layer, and later layers always win.
`green` — two `!important` rules tie, so source order decides.
Neither — `!important` on both sides cancels out to the default color.

questions.title: An unlayered rule and a layered rule conflict — which wins?

options.text: `red` — unlayered normal declarations rank above any layer, regardless of source order.
`green` — it is inside an explicit layer, and layers always beat unlayered rules.
`green` — the layered rule appears later in the source, so it wins the tie.
`red` — only because it is written first; swapping the order would flip it.

questions.explanation:
Declarations that aren't inside any `@layer` are gathered into an **implicit layer that sits *after* all named layers** — so for **normal** declarations, *unlayered beats layered*. This is a matter of layer precedence, **not source order**: the unlayered rule here wins even though it appears *first* in the file.

Both selectors are `a` at specificity (0,0,1), but layer precedence is checked before specificity and before order, so the **unlayered** rule wins → the link is **red**. Swapping the two blocks would not change the outcome.

```css
a { color: red; }                   /* unlayered → highest-priority normal band */
@layer base { a { color: green; } } /* layered → loses despite coming later */
```

(For `!important` this reverses too: unlayered important declarations have the *lowest* priority among author important declarations.)

questions.title: Does the stylesheet override this inline style?
questions.description:
Pick the color the paragraph ends up with. ```html

Hello

``` ```css #msg { color: green !important; } ```

options.text: `green` — an `!important` author rule outranks an inline style, which is only a normal declaration.
`blue` — an inline `style` attribute always wins over any stylesheet rule.
`blue` — the inline style and `#msg` tie on specificity, so the inline one wins.
`green` — because `#msg` is more specific than an inline style.

questions.explanation:
Importance outranks the inline `style` attribute. The cascade splits author declarations into *normal* and *important* bands, with important sitting **above** normal. An inline style is just a high-priority **normal** declaration, so an `!important` author rule lives in a higher band and wins. The text is **green**. ```html

Hello

``` ```css #msg { color: green !important; } /* important author rule beats inline normal */ ``` Without `!important`, the inline `blue` would win — inline styles outrank ordinary selector specificity for normal declarations. Adding `!important` in the stylesheet is the one author tool that overrides an inline style (short of putting `!important` *on* the inline declaration itself).

questions.title: Which of these properties inherit by default?
questions.description: Select every property whose value flows to descendant elements without an explicit `inherit`.

options.text: `color`
`font-family`
`cursor`
`padding`
`border-width`

questions.explanation:
Inheritance is decided per property by its specification. The rule of thumb: **text- and font-related properties inherit** so typography flows down the tree, while **box-model and layout properties do not** — otherwise every child would duplicate its parent's padding and borders.

- `color` — inherited ✔
- `font-family` — inherited ✔
- `cursor` — inherited ✔ (a non-text property that still inherits, so a `cursor` set on a container applies to its descendants)
- `padding` — **not** inherited ✘
- `border-width` — **not** inherited ✘

Other commonly inherited properties: `line-height`, `letter-spacing`, `text-align`, `white-space`, `visibility`, `list-style`. Not inherited: `margin`, `background`, `width`, `height`, `display`, `position`. You can always force the issue with the `inherit` keyword on a non-inherited property.

questions.title: What is the computed `display` of a `<div>` with `display: initial`?
questions.description:
Pick the computed value.

```css
div { display: initial; }
```

options.text: `inline` — `initial` resets to the property's spec initial value, which for `display` is `inline`.
`block` — `initial` restores the browser's default for a `div`.
`inline-block` — the compromise between the spec default and the UA default.
`unset` — the declaration is invalid and is ignored.

questions.explanation:
`initial` resets a property to the **initial value defined in its specification** — *not* the browser's default for that element. The spec initial value of `display` is **`inline`**. What normally makes a `div` block-level is the user-agent stylesheet rule `div { display: block; }`, and `initial` deliberately ignores the UA stylesheet. So `display: initial` makes the `div` compute to **`inline`**.

```css
div { display: initial; } /* computed display: inline, not block */
```

This trips people up because they read `initial` as "the default I'm used to seeing." It actually means "this property's own spec default," and for `display` that default is `inline` for every element.

questions.title: And with `display: revert` on the same `<div>`?
questions.description:
Pick the statement that correctly describes `revert` versus `initial`.

```css
div { display: revert; }
```

options.text: `block` — `revert` rolls back to the user-agent stylesheet (`div { display: block }`), whereas `initial` would give `inline`.
`inline` — `revert` and `initial` are synonyms for the spec initial value.
`inline-block` — `revert` averages the UA default with the spec default.
It is invalid — `revert` is not a real CSS-wide keyword.

questions.explanation:
`revert` is **not** a synonym for `initial`. It rolls the property back to the value it would have had from the **previous cascade origin**. For an author declaration, the previous origin is the user (or, lacking that, the user-agent) stylesheet — where `div { display: block; }` lives. So `display: revert` makes the `div` compute to **`block`**.

Contrast the two keywords directly:

```css
div { display: revert;  } /* block  — rolls back to the UA stylesheet */
div { display: initial; } /* inline — the property's spec initial value */
```

Reach for `revert` when you mean "undo my styles and let the browser defaults stand"; reach for `initial` when you want the property's spec default regardless of element.

questions.title: Which CSS-wide keyword acts as `inherit` for inherited properties and `initial` otherwise?
questions.description: Enter the single keyword (the chameleon of the four CSS-wide keywords).
questions.explanation:
`unset` is the chameleon keyword: it behaves as **`inherit` for properties that inherit by default**, and as **`initial` for properties that do not**.

```css
.card {
  color: unset;  /* color inherits → behaves like inherit */
  border: unset; /* border does not inherit → behaves like initial (no border) */
}
```

The four CSS-wide keywords compared:

- `inherit` — always take the parent's computed value.
- `initial` — always the property's spec initial value.
- `unset` — inherit-or-initial, depending on whether the property inherits.
- `revert` — roll back to the previous cascade origin (e.g. the UA stylesheet).

`all: unset` applies this to *every* property at once — a quick way to neutralize both inherited and default styling on an element.
